tech.headline: China develops heavy‑liquid recycling for silicon modules

Life‑cycle analysis shows an 80.42% carbon‑emission reduction versus conventional methods and positive returns from solar‑cell and solder‑strip stages.

learn.title: Rooftop Solar & Net Metering in India
learn.intro: Rooftop solar lets households generate clean power, reducing grid load and bills. In India, net metering policies let owners sell excess electricity back to utilities, accelerating the renewable transition and empowering consumers.
learn.c1_text: Photovoltaic cells convert sunlight into DC electricity. Imagine each cell as a tiny rain‑collector; when photons hit, they free electrons that flow like water through a pipe, producing about 150 W per square metre under peak sun.
learn.c2_text: The DC from panels passes through an inverter, turning it into AC that matches grid voltage. The system syncs with the utility, so when your house uses less than it generates, the surplus flows outward automatically.
learn.c3_text: Under MNRE guidelines, consumers can offset up to 100 % of their consumption. States such as Tamil Nadu and Gujarat offer 1:1 credit, and tariffs are set by the distribution company, encouraging over 40 GW of rooftop capacity by 2024.
learn.stat4: Savings: up to 60 % on electricity bills

learn.title: Vehicle‑to‑Grid (V2G) Power Balance
learn.intro: Electric vehicles store large amounts of energy. V2G technology lets them act as mobile batteries, feeding power back to the grid during peaks and helping stabilise renewable fluctuations.
learn.c1_title: EV Battery as a Grid Asset ⚡
learn.c1_text: A typical 60 kWh EV battery can supply 10 kW for one hour—enough to power a small office. Think of each car as a backup generator that can be called on when the sun dips or wind stalls.
learn.c2_text: Bidirectional chargers allow electricity to flow both ways. They communicate with grid operators via smart‑grid protocols, dispatching power in 5‑minute intervals to smooth demand spikes.
learn.c3_text: IRENA notes India plans 30 GW of EVs by 2030. Pilot projects in Delhi and Bangalore are testing V2G, aiming to integrate up to 1 GW of vehicle‑derived flexibility by 2035.
learn.stat1: Global EVs with V2G capability: 5 million (2023)
learn.stat2: Potential V2G capacity in India: 12 GW by 2030
learn.stat4: Cost saving for grid operators: up to $30 /MWh

learn.title: Power Purchase Agreements Explained
learn.intro: A Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) is a contract that guarantees revenue for renewable projects, making them bankable. It connects developers with corporate or utility off‑takers, unlocking financing for clean energy.
learn.c1_text: Under a PPA, the buyer agrees to purchase electricity at a pre‑set price (e.g., $0.05 /kWh) for 10‑25 years. This certainty is like a lease that lets banks lend money at lower interest rates.
learn.c2_text: The developer bears construction risk, while the off‑taker takes market price risk. Performance guarantees ensure the plant delivers a minimum output, often 90 % of expected generation.
learn.c3_text: Indian firms such as Tata Power and Infosys have signed PPAs for 1‑2 GW of solar. The MNRE’s “green‑power” guidelines streamline approvals, boosting corporate renewable procurement by 40 % in 2023.
learn.stat4: Financing cost reduction: up to 30 % vs merchant sales

learn.title: Second‑Life EV Batteries for Storage
learn.intro: When EV batteries retire from vehicle use (≈80 % capacity), they still hold enough energy for stationary storage, extending value and reducing waste while supporting grid resilience.
learn.c1_text: A 70 kWh EV battery at 80 % SOC still stores 56 kWh—enough for a 100 kW micro‑grid for half a day. Repurposing is like giving a used laptop a new job as a home server.
learn.c2_text: Battery Management Systems are re‑programmed for longer cycle life (2,000‑3,000 cycles). Power electronics are resized for stationary use, and thermal management shifts from active cooling to passive designs.
learn.c3_text: Delhi Metro and Karnataka Power Corporation are testing second‑life packs for 10 MW of peak‑shaving. IRENA estimates India could redeploy 5‑7 GWh of used EV batteries by 2030.
learn.stat2: Cost per kWh for repurposed battery: $120
learn.stat4: CO₂ avoided: 0.5 t per MWh stored

learn.title: Solar Panel Types Compared
learn.intro: Choosing the right solar panel impacts efficiency, cost, and space needs. Understanding monocrystalline, polycrystalline, and thin‑film technologies helps installers and homeowners make informed decisions.
learn.c1_text: Made from a single silicon crystal, these panels reach 22‑24 % efficiency. Imagine a tightly packed bookshelf; each cell captures maximum sunlight, ideal for limited roof space.
learn.c2_text: Multiple silicon crystals form the wafer, yielding 15‑18 % efficiency. It’s like a looser bookshelf—more cells per panel but slightly lower output, making it cheaper per watt.
learn.c3_text: Uses materials like CdTe or perovskite, achieving 10‑13 % efficiency on flexible substrates. Perfect for building‑integrated PV in India’s hot climates where weight and aesthetics matter.

learn.title: India’s National Green Hydrogen Mission
learn.intro: Green hydrogen, produced from renewable electricity, offers a zero‑carbon fuel for industry and transport. India’s mission sets ambitious targets and incentives to build a domestic hydrogen economy.
learn.c1_text: The mission aims for 5 million metric tonnes of green hydrogen annually by 2030, equivalent to the output of 10 large coal power plants, to decarbonise steel, fertilizers and transport.
learn.c2_text: Capital subsidies up to 30 % and a production‑linked incentive of $2‑$3 per kg are offered. Tax exemptions on equipment and priority grid access further lower project costs.
learn.c3_text: Pilot electrolyser plants in Gujarat and Rajasthan, backed by NTPC and private players, aim for 1 GW renewable capacity each. By 2025, India expects 10 GW of renewable power dedicated to hydrogen.
learn.stat2: Planned green hydrogen electrolyser capacity: 15 GW (by 2030)
learn.stat3: Projected cost of green H₂: $2.5 /kg by 2030
